audit_status.source_base: Hebrew Deuteronomy / Devarim mourning, food distinction, and tithe law, draft aligned later
audit_status.review_note: First-pass draft reviewed for mourning-body practices, clean/unclean land and water animals, birds, carrion, kid-in-milk prohibition, annual tithe pilgrimage, monetary conversion, Levite inclusion, and third-year tithe for vulnerable residents.

verses.restored: Children are you to YHWH your Divine. You shall not cut yourselves or make baldness between your eyes for the dead.
For a holy people are you to YHWH your Divine, and YHWH chose you to be for him a treasured people from all peoples upon face of ground.
You shall not eat any abominable thing.
These are animals you may eat: ox, sheep, and goat,
deer, gazelle, roebuck, wild goat, ibex, antelope, and mountain sheep.
Every animal dividing hoof, cleaving two clefts, and chewing cud among animals, it you may eat.
Yet these you shall not eat among those chewing cud or dividing cleft hoof: camel, hare, and rock badger, for they chew cud but do not divide hoof; unclean they are for you.
And pig, for it divides hoof but does not chew cud; unclean it is for you. From their flesh you shall not eat, and their carcass you shall not touch.
These you may eat from all in waters: everything having fins and scales you may eat.
Whatever has no fins and scales you shall not eat; unclean it is for you.
Every clean bird you may eat.
But these are those you shall not eat: eagle, vulture, black vulture,
red kite, falcon, and kite according to its kind,
every raven according to its kind,
ostrich, owl, gull, and hawk according to its kind,
little owl, great owl, white owl,
pelican, carrion vulture, cormorant,
stork, heron according to its kind, hoopoe, and bat.
Every winged swarming thing is unclean for you; they shall not be eaten.
Every clean winged creature you may eat.

You shall not eat anything that dies of itself. To sojourner within your gates you may give it and he may eat it, or you may sell it to foreigner, for a holy people are you to YHWH your Divine. You shall not boil a kid in milk of its mother.
You shall surely tithe all produce of your seed coming out of field year by year.

You shall eat before YHWH your Divine, in place he chooses to cause his name to dwell, tithe of grain, new wine, oil, and firstborn of herd and flock, so you may learn to fear/revere YHWH your Divine all days.
If way is too long for you, so you are not able to carry it, because place YHWH your Divine chooses to set his name there is too far from you, when YHWH your Divine blesses you,
then you shall turn it into silver, bind silver in your hand, and go to place YHWH your Divine chooses.

You shall give silver for whatever your soul/life desires—for cattle, flock, wine, strong drink, or anything your soul/life asks—and you shall eat there before YHWH your Divine and rejoice, you and your household.
The Levite within your gates you shall not abandon, for he has no portion or inheritance with you.
At end of three years you shall bring out all tithe of your produce in that year and place it within your gates.

Then Levite, because he has no portion or inheritance with you, and sojourner, orphan, and widow within your gates shall come and eat and be satisfied, so YHWH your Divine may bless you in all work of your hand that you do.

translation_notes: Mourning practices are regulated as identity markers; the law must not shame grief, hair loss, scars, cultural mourning, or people who self-injure and need care.
The food list belongs to Israel's dietary holiness tradition and later Jewish kashrut; it must not be mocked, superseded, or imposed on non-Jews.
Species identifications for several birds are uncertain and require lexical/zoological audit.
The rule concerning carrion distinguishes Israelite holiness practice from sojourner/foreigner food economy; it should not be used to demean immigrants.
The pilgrimage tithe includes practical monetary conversion and household rejoicing.
The third-year tithe turns agricultural wealth into local food security for Levite, immigrant, orphan, and widow.
safety_note: Do not use Deuteronomy 14 to stigmatize grieving bodies, shame eating practices, demean Jewish kashrut, or treat immigrants as inferior. Center holiness as disciplined identity and tithing as shared food, joy, and material provision for vulnerable neighbors.
